notes: A real, confirmed advantage over Chattanooga/Hamilton County's equivalent check — the documented reason Asheville's Room for others score sits above Chattanooga's

fact_label: Asheville-specific Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace premium
notes: The national $687-750 figure is not location-specific

Real, material conflict with crowdsourced-aggregator framing, which paints Asheville as notably above-average; the Chamber's own PDF cites a 95.6-106.2 range dating to 1990 — historically always near the national middle
Absolute-level crime-rate sourcing conflicts by up to 2.5x depending on methodology, but the improving trajectory holds regardless of which baseline is used

value_raw: Mission Hospital, inside city limits, only Level I trauma center in all of Western North Carolina (ACS-verified December 2025); 94 ER beds inside an 853-bed hospital, region's only dedicated pediatric ER
notes: missionhealth.org, bpr.org — no distance gap to name, sits inside the city itself, the strongest of USA's three locations on this specific measure. Corrects a prior [GAP] row

AVL Digital Nomads, dedicated coworking spaces, and nomad-guide-site profiles all confirm a real, organic scene — but no quantified resident/remote-worker count was found, unlike Mérida's (4,000-6,000) or Oaxaca's (~22,700) sourced figures

Local reporting explicitly names displacement risk to "established residents and artists" — same mechanism as foreign-driven gentrification elsewhere, just without the "anti-gringo" framing since it's domestic
43 dead in Buncombe County alone; recovery real but incomplete — reservoir fuse gate not fully repaired until fall 2026, ~130 people still homeless
Whether an Asheville-area parcel sits within 50 miles of a military installation has not been checked, since the underlying law isn't in effect yet.

A separate Canopy MLS (real closed-transaction) figure runs materially higher ($452,550 median, $569,448 mean) — a genuine, unreconciled sourcing conflict that makes the budget-fit problem look worse, not better
Down 2.86% year-over-year (softening trend, not a sharp drop)

Flagged as possibly a non-issue under the earlier working assumption (domestic relocation, no customs crossing) — that assumption is corrected as of 2026-07-08: an actual international customs crossing likely IS the real scenario here, not a non-issue. Still genuinely unresearched either way — flagging the corrected premise, not assuming the answer
No dedicated search done on NC's political climate or culture-war-adjacent local ordinances
Buncombe County-wide 2026 reassessment; property tax here is not a flat, predictable number
5 sources genuinely disagree by a wide band
Two independent searches converge — what exists in the sub-$150k tier is manufactured housing or vacant land, not a comparable standalone home
Confirmed no looser county-level option, unlike Buncombe County's real exception — the documented reason Chattanooga's score sits below Asheville's

165+ listings citywide under $150k, including a real 3BR/1BA bungalow at $69,900 — the closest realistic in-budget match found anywhere in USA's research; caveat: correlates specifically with a historically disinvested neighborhood, not a like-for-like discount

fact_label: Chattanooga-specific Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace premium
notes: Same national-figure-only gap as Asheville
Reads as genuinely calmer day-to-day, but rests on a thinner dedicated search than Asheville's — not confirmed-clean
No offsetting improving-trend data found the way Asheville's APD 2025 report provided

value_raw: Erlanger Baroness Hospital, inside downtown Chattanooga, the only Level I trauma center serving a four-state region (SE Tennessee, N Georgia, N Alabama, W North Carolina); ~53,000 ER visits/year
notes: erlanger.org — sits inside the city itself, both mainland USA locations clear this measure cleanly, a genuine point of contrast against Rincón's 30-40 minute buffer. Corrects a prior [GAP] row
Vs. 12.5% national; +2 points 2022-2025; 32% of workers hold jobs paying below the local cost-of-basics threshold despite a real manufacturing jobs boom
Chattanooga's own community-network.md wasn't checked for a countable-population figure this pass

fact_label: Location applicability — Tennessee's foreign land-ownership law (HB1788/SB2424)
notes: A statewide Tennessee law; Chattanooga sits inside it by definition.
Genuinely below Asheville's range at either end of either city's own spread
Vs. North Carolina's flat 3.99% (Asheville) — a genuine, structural cost-of-living difference
13% below the US national average overall; housing specifically 23% below
Same reasoning as Asheville's row
Same gap as Asheville's
Numbeo and Expatistan genuinely agree once compared like-for-like (~4% apart on an all-in basis)
Structural legal exclusion, not a pricing quirk — Puerto Rico residents cannot use healthcare.gov at all

fact_label: Act 60 Individual Resident Investor accepts pre-existing passive income
notes: The 0% treatment applies only to gains/income accrued after establishing PR residency, not pre-existing passive income; the separate Export Services track (services billed to non-PR clients) is the only route that could apply to pre-existing income, and only if restructured to meet that track's own requirements

fact_label: Act 60 Individual Resident Investor decree — application window closing
notes: Applicants after Dec 31, 2026 face a new 4% tax on post-residency investment income instead of 0%, plus a 6-year non-PR-residency look-back

33% energy shortfall in 2025; Hurricane Fiona (2022) knocked out the grid for up to 4 weeks in parts of the island — a chronic, not one-time, vulnerability, "suspended between two realities" per a top regulator

Worse statistically than either mainland location, but qualitative sources describe it as generally safe day-to-day — the same "worse on paper, calmer in practice" pattern this project flagged for Lagos
No 24-hour ER in Rincón itself; nearest is Mayagüez — meaningfully shorter than Atitlán's 2.5-3 hour buffer, but not "there's a hospital in town"
No quantified count of Act-60 arrivals or mainland transplants found for Rincón specifically, unlike Mérida's or Oaxaca's sourced figures

Rincón's organized backlash (row above) is a social/political mechanism (protest, messaging); this is a distinct criminal-targeting mechanism, not searched for separately — the two shouldn't be read as one finding covering both
Puerto Rico is not North Carolina and not reached by this state bill either way.

fact_label: Location applicability — Tennessee's foreign land-ownership law (HB1788/SB2424)
notes: Puerto Rico is not a US state and is not reached by a Tennessee state law — never inherits the mainland finding either way.
Act 60 arrivals directly named as backlash targets in national reporting; the sharpest organized social-conflict finding anywhere in USA's research
Given the domestic-travel framing above, a cat-specific divergence is less likely than for an international candidate, but not yet confirmed either way

fact_label: Pet import (dog) — rabies titer / quarantine requirement
value_raw: Domestic travel, not international — standard rabies/health certificate, microchip, parasite treatment, no quarantine, no import permit
notes: Puerto Rico is domestic US travel for pet purposes — genuinely simpler than Guatemala's or Mexico's requirements

$76B in restructuring savings, first balanced budget passed June 2025, but three more required before the board's mandate can even conclude. criterion_id corrected 2026-07-15 (was previously missing entirely, despite this being the file's own explicitly-named political-institutional-stability section)
Currently favorable relative to Asheville's reassessment shock, but PR's own fiscal oversight board has flagged the system as needing an overhaul — a low bill today isn't necessarily stable long-term
Not closed with a dedicated search the way Asheville's and Chattanooga's both were; the overall price level suggests this gap may be even more material here

value_raw: The federal administration attempted to dismiss three members of the fiscal oversight board (PROMESA) (Biggs, González, Rosa) — a body PROMESA itself designed to be independent of exactly this kind of executive interference. All three provisionally reinstated October 2025 after prevailing in a lawsuit arguing they'd been removed without the "just cause" PROMESA requires
notes: A genuinely novel institutional-handover fight — whether a sitting federal administration can simply remove an independent fiscal board's own members — layered on top of, not the same question as, the board's underlying austerity mandate

fact_label: Fiscal oversight board's own transparency failure
value_raw: As of January 2026, the board had gone a full year without publishing members' legally-required financial disclosures — roughly 50 missing filings, including 33 quarterly reports for Jan-Sept 2025 and 11 annual 2024 reports
notes: Centro de Periodismo Investigativo. A body whose entire mandate is fiscal oversight failing its own disclosure obligations is itself a live governance-quality signal, independent of the austerity-vs-crime axis

value_raw: Governor Jenniffer González-Colón (PNP, sworn in January 2025, term through 2028) has her own administration embroiled in a corruption controversy as of June 2026 — allegations of improper government-contracting interference and political patronage centered on staff member Francisco Domenech
notes: Current, not historical, as of this research

fact_label: Statehood-status question — genuinely stalled, not a live risk
value_raw: The Puerto Rico Status Act (which would have set a binding plebiscite for November 2, 2025) never passed Congress — passed the House once in 2022 in a different form, died in the Senate; reintroduced in the 118th Congress, never reached a floor vote in either chamber
notes: No plebiscite happened on the schedule some 2024-era coverage described, and none is currently scheduled — this axis reads as a genuinely stalled, low-tempo political question, not the live governor/legislature-level volatility the fiscal-oversight and corruption findings represent. Named plainly so the real active risks aren't diluted by the most dramatic-sounding axis actually being the stalled one

fact_label: Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace access for lawfully-present non-immigrant visa holders
notes: Confirmed specifically for H-1B and E-2: neither carries an insurance mandate as a condition of status, and holders are "lawfully present" for marketplace-purchase purposes (healthcare.gov, nnuimmigration.com) — market access itself is not gated by visa category

fact_label: Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace age-based enrollment ceiling
notes: A genuine structural strength relative to several other candidates' private markets, where new-enrollment age ceilings commonly land 65-75 — largely moot in practice given the Medicare-access finding above

fact_label: Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace subsidy eligibility below 100% federal poverty line
notes: A second, earlier subsidy-narrowing date, independent of the 2027 lawful-permanent-resident (LPR)-only change above

fact_label: Affordable Care Act (ACA) Marketplace subsidy eligibility for visa holders who are not lawful permanent residents (LPR)
notes: Narrows to lawful permanent residents (LPR) plus a short named-exceptions list (certain Cuban/Haitian immigrants, COFA migrants); every real non-LPR Framing-B route in visa-legal.md loses subsidy eligibility on this date — market access itself remains (see row above), only affordability narrows

Whether a given applicant's income sits above or below this line determines Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidy eligibility — the single biggest swing factor in this cost category across all three USA locations

value_raw: Not tolerated — a visible pattern is itself a risk trigger
notes: Named explicitly as this project's "notably stricter than most candidates" case: no CA-4-style shared clock or tolerated workaround exists; U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officers can deny re-entry or shorten admission on suspicion of a repeat short-exit pattern
Lowest closing-cost range of any candidate in this project

The headline Framing-B finding, unchanged: unlike Guatemala, Colombia, Argentina, Portugal, Greece, and Mexico — each of which has some passive-income/remote-work category — the US has never created one. The flip side, now developed: most real routes that do exist convert directly to permanent residency, unlike a renewable-forever nomad visa

value_raw: ~55,000 green cards/year, random lottery, excludes high-volume source countries
notes: Named for completeness, not developed as a plannable route; whether this project's own candidate countries are excluded by recent admission volumes was not checked country-by-country — a real, named gap

fact_label: E-2 Treaty Investor — nationality-restricted (treaty country required)
notes: The applicant's own nationality, not residence, gates eligibility entirely — a structural cap the EB-5, National Interest Waiver (NIW), and EB-1A routes don't share

fact_label: E-2 Treaty Investor — qualifying investment threshold
notes: No fixed statutory minimum — a "substantial," at-risk investment scaled to the business. Genuinely the lower-capital investor option this pass was asked to find, well below EB-5's floor

fact_label: E-2 Treaty Investor converts to permanent residency
notes: Renewable indefinitely as a non-immigrant status but does not itself lead to a green card — the honest catch behind the lower capital bar; a green card requires separately qualifying under EB-5, EB-1C, or another category

fact_label: EB-1A Extraordinary Ability — self-petition, no employer or labor certification (PERM) required
notes: Requires sustained national/international acclaim (3 of 10 evidentiary criteria); converts directly to a green card, same immigrant-category shape as the National Interest Waiver (NIW) but a materially higher evidentiary bar — "very top of the field," not just a strong professional record

fact_label: EB-2 India — final action date backlog
notes: The single most easily-overlooked fact in this whole section: identical category, identical filing date, wildly different outcome by country of birth (not citizenship or residence)

fact_label: EB-2 National Interest Waiver (NIW) — self-petition, no employer or labor certification (PERM) required
notes: Requires advanced degree or exceptional ability, plus the Dhanasar 3-prong national-interest test; converts directly to a green card (this IS the immigrant category, not a temporary status). Best fit for a professional/research-track-record profile, a weak fit for pure passive/rental income with no distinguishing achievement

fact_label: EB-2 National Interest Waiver (NIW) — I-140 premium processing fee (2026)
notes: Effective March 1, 2026; buys a 45-business-day U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) adjudication commitment. Standard processing ran 2.5-26.5 months for 80% of cases as of June 2026

fact_label: EB-2/EB-3 backlog — rest of world (most nationalities, including this project's own candidate countries)
notes: The large majority of the world's nationalities, including Guatemalan-, Colombian-, Moroccan-, and Thai-born applicants, face no comparable structural wait — a real, material "which passport" fact

fact_label: EB-3 India — final action date backlog
notes: Same per-country-cap mechanism as EB-2 India above

fact_label: EB-5 Investor Visa — qualifying investment threshold
notes: $800k in a Targeted Employment Area (rural/high-unemployment) or qualifying infrastructure project, $1.05M elsewhere; U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) states these hold through FY2026, inflation-indexed increase expected from January 2027 (trajectory qualifier: stable through FY2026 specifically)

fact_label: EB-5 Investor Visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
notes: An asset/capital-investment test, not an income test — same shape as Guatemala's Investor Visa row

fact_label: EB-5 Investor Visa converts to permanent residency
notes: 2-year conditional green card first, I-829 petition to remove conditions once the 10-job requirement is proven, then unconditional; citizenship-eligible after 5 years total residency
Sidesteps the individual-marketplace question for employer-sponsored routes specifically; does not apply to self-petition routes (EB-2 NIW, EB-1A) or E-2's typical sole-proprietor shape
Passed a House committee vote, referred to House Rules/Calendar/Operations Committee as of this pass.
This pass did not read the enacted statute text itself (TCA Title 66, Ch. 2, Pt. 3 as amended) — reconstructed from corroborating 2026 news/legislative-tracking sources.

fact_label: Family sponsorship — F1 preference (unmarried adult child of a citizen) backlog
notes: Real, converts directly to permanent residency, but genuinely too slow to plan a move around for most applicants

fact_label: Family sponsorship — F3 preference (married child of a citizen) backlog
notes: A Filipino family that filed in 2005 only saw its priority date become current in April 2026 — a real 21-year worked example, not a projection

fact_label: Family sponsorship — immediate relative of a US citizen (spouse/parent/minor child)
value_raw: No cap, no backlog (~12-24 months all-in)
notes: Genuinely one of the fastest, least-capital routes in the entire system — requires an actual qualifying relationship, not creatable on demand

China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria recur across enacted state laws; mostly scoped to agricultural land, critical-infrastructure proximity, or land near military bases, not a blanket ban — narrower than Guatemala's/Mexico's restricted zones but real and nationality-dependent, a fact Framing A's "no restriction at all" summary couldn't surface

fact_label: H-1B — supplemental fee for consular-processing petitions
notes: Applies only when the beneficiary is outside the US at filing; change-of-status filings from inside the US (e.g. F-1 to H-1B) are exempt — an easily-missed distinction
H-1B is a dual-intent visa — pursuing a green card doesn't jeopardize the underlying status, unlike a B-visa

fact_label: H-1B employer-sponsored visa — lottery selection odds (FY2027)
notes: New wage-weighted lottery: ~15% at entry-level (Level I) wage up to ~46%+ at the top (Level IV) tier — not a single flat probability. Requires an actual US employer/specialty-occupation job offer, not a fit for remote/passive income alone
Projected +8% further in 2026; high wind-risk areas +58%

fact_label: International Entrepreneur Rule — status type and duration
value_raw: Parole (not a visa), up to 2.5 years, renewable once to 5-year max
notes: Discretionary, revocable, no formal immigration status; a new $1,000 parole fee applies as of October 16, 2025

fact_label: International Entrepreneur Rule converts to permanent residency
notes: The lowest personal-capital bar of any route in this file, and the most fragile — parole recipients generally must separately qualify under another category, sometimes after leaving the US, to ever adjust to permanent residency

fact_label: International Entrepreneur Rule (IER) — qualifying startup investment
notes: No personal-investment minimum — the startup itself must meet this bar. Largest update since the program's 2017 launch

This mechanism (as distinct from organized social backlash, which Rincón's file does document) doesn't apply the same way it does at every other candidate in this project — a US citizen relocating domestically isn't a foreign national in a local market. Stated explicitly as N/A rather than left silent

fact_label: L-1A intracompany transferee → EB-1C — converts to permanent residency
notes: Typical timeline 3-5 years for most nationalities, among the faster employment-based routes — but only usable by someone who already owns/executively runs a genuine, operating foreign company capable of a real US affiliate; not a route buildable from scratch
KFF; includes credit-card, family-loan, and direct provider medical debt

unit: % of personal bankruptcy filings citing a medical factor
notes: Consumer Bankruptcy Project survey data via Debt.org/RetireGuide compilations — a widely-cited but methodologically disputed figure in health-economics literature; cited as directionally real, not a precise causal share

fact_label: Medicare eligibility for a non-lawful-permanent-resident (LPR) Framing-B visa holder
notes: Medicare requires lawful permanent resident (LPR) status (plus 5 years' continuous residency, or a 40-quarter US work history) and, since July 2025, is further restricted to citizens/LPRs/named exceptions only — no "aging into safety net" path exists for a non-LPR holder regardless of age
Largest single-year FBI-recorded drop ever (2021→2024); 2025 estimate would be the lowest ever recorded since 1900

O-1 is a real non-immigrant bridge to EB-1A/National Interest Waiver (NIW) in practice but requires an employer/agent sponsor unlike the two self-petition routes above; whether it permits dual intent without jeopardizing status was not confirmed

fact_label: Public/universal healthcare system for a working-age non-citizen
notes: No NHS-style public system; Medicare (65+) and Medicaid (low-income) are the only public programs, and neither is generally available to a non-lawful-permanent-resident (LPR) Framing-B visa holder — see the Medicare row below

SB 394 ('Prohibit Foreign Ownership of NC Land') — narrower than Tennessee's even if enacted: agricultural land and property within a 50-mile radius of a military installation only, not a blanket residential ban.

The National Security and Economic Protection Act (HB1788/SB2424), cross-filed 2026-05-04, effective 2026-07-01 — the 2026 amendment extends the restriction to individuals, reaching ordinary non-agricultural homes/condos/vacant land.

value_raw: Legal US residents generally exempt, unless the purchase is funded from foreign-controlled sources
notes: Renders unevaluable without the deferred residency-status+funds-source profile field — informational, never a gate, until that field lands.
Open-ended qualifier ('and other federally-designated adversarial countries') means this list is not confirmed exhaustive.

value_raw: CN, CU, IR, KP, RU, VE
notes: China, Cuba, Iran, North Korea, Russia, and Venezuela's Maduro regime — Syria NOT confirmed covered under Tennessee's specific list.

fact_label: Standard EB-2/EB-3 (PERM, employer-sponsored) converts to permanent residency
notes: Immigrant category by construction — the per-country backlog below is a wait for an already-guaranteed outcome, not a risk of denial
Full fee-simple ownership, subject to the nationality-specific exception above.

value_raw: 180d-1yr: 3-year bar; 1yr+: 10-year bar; Visa Waiver Program (VWP) overstay: possible permanent VWP ineligibility + no right to contest removal before a judge
notes: Visa Waiver Program (VWP) overstay carries a harsher structural consequence than a B-visa overstay — a real asymmetry between the two visitor tracks

fact_label: Tourist/visitor stay length — B-1/B-2 (non-VWP nationalities, the majority of the world)
notes: Covers most nationalities, including every one of this project's own candidate countries; visa document itself often valid up to 10 years multiple-entry, but each admitted stay is set by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer at entry, typically up to 6 months

fact_label: Tourist/visitor stay length — Visa Waiver Program (VWP/ESTA) (42 countries)
notes: Automatic on arrival via the Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA), no consular interview, for ~42 countries (most of Western Europe, UK, Japan, South Korea, Australia, NZ, Chile, Singapore, Taiwan, Israel, others)

fact_label: Tourist/visitor time counts toward anything longer-term
notes: Sharper than the standard "doesn't count" default found elsewhere: nonimmigrant intent is an active, sworn requirement, so filing for a green card too soon after a tourist entry can itself be read as evidence of misrepresented intent at entry — a fraud-adjacent risk, not just a wasted clock

value_raw: Visa Waiver Program (VWP): none (narrow 30-day medical exception only); B-2: one, up to +6 months
notes: B-2 extension filed via Form I-539, 45+ days before expiry, discretionary, requires a legitimate reason; no second extension except extraordinary circumstances

fact_label: Unsubsidized Affordable Care Act (ACA) marketplace premium (40yo)
notes: Roughly doubled after enhanced subsidies expired Dec 31, 2025 — a real, quantified, recent cost shift with no equivalent anywhere else in this project

fact_label: North Carolina Supreme Court election contest (2024 race) — a resolved institutional stress test
value_raw: 734-vote margin (Griffin vs. incumbent Justice Riggs); Griffin challenged 65,000+ ballots as retroactively ineligible for ~6 months; a federal judge (Trump appointee) rejected the challenge, Griffin conceded May 7, 2025, Riggs certified to serve her 8-year term
notes: Votebeat, Brennan Center, AP/CNBC. A real, documented attempt to alter an electoral/judicial outcome after the fact — but the state's own courts and process actually held, unlike a case where an executive or legislature simply overrode a result. Worth tracking as a precedent (the tactic could recur in a future close race), not an ongoing unresolved condition today

fact_label: North Carolina "Farmland and Military Protection Act" (HB 133) — pending, not yet confirmed signed
value_raw: Prohibits an "adversarial foreign government" — including nationals of a "country of concern" who aren't US citizens or LPRs — from purchasing, leasing, or holding an interest in agricultural land, or land within a 75-mile radius of a military installation; presented to the Governor 2026-07-02, applies retroactively to acquisitions from Dec 1, 2025 onward if enacted
notes: Legislative Reporting Service, ncleg.gov. Narrowly scoped (named adversarial nations only — China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, similar) and wouldn't touch most nationalities — but a real, current example of the foreign-investment-posture half of this criterion's test, not hypothetical

fact_label: Tennessee mid-decade congressional redistricting — a 50-year institutional norm reversed in 6 days
value_raw: Since 1972, Tennessee law barred the legislature from redrawing congressional districts between decennial apportionments. Following SCOTUS's April 29, 2026 ruling in Louisiana v. Callais, Gov. Bill Lee called a special session May 1, 2026; the 1972 bar was repealed and a new map (carving Memphis/Shelby County across three districts) both passed and were signed by May 7, 2026 — six calendar days
notes: Ballotpedia, Wikipedia, Tennessee Lookout. Tennessee NAACP has since sued challenging the new map (filed May 7, state's response May 11, 2026) — a live, open legal fight, not a settled outcome unlike Asheville's own Supreme Court contest which actually concluded

fact_label: Tennessee foreign-land-ownership restriction (HB 40) — settled law, not new churn
value_raw: Prohibits "nonresident aliens," foreign businesses, and foreign governments from a "country of concern" from purchasing real property in the state (including agricultural land, water rights, easements) — signed into law May 11, 2023, one of roughly 36 US states with an equivalent law
notes: Holland & Knight, National Ag Law Center. Named for completeness — this has been settled law for three years, unlike North Carolina's still-pending HB 133 equivalent
